bibl.author: Phillips, Watts
bibl.title: A Golden Fetter
An Original Drama in three acts
Lacy's Acting Edition, volume 83, No. 1245

event.desc: Premiered at Royal Holborn Theatre 17 Feb. 1869

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible

docTitle.titlePart: An Original Drama in THREE ACTS.

titlePage.byline: By WATTS PHILLIPS, Esq., AUTHOR OF The Poor Strollers, Camilla's Husband, His Last Victory, A Ticket of Leave,Paul's Return, Huguenot Captain, Nobody's Child, Land Rats and Water Rats, Theodora, Lost in London, Woman in Mauve, Paper Wings, Maud's Peril, Story of the '45, Not Guilty, The Dead Heart, Lion at Bay, &c, &c.
titlePage.docImprint: THOMAS HAILES LACY, THEATRICAL PUBLISHER, LONDON.

performance.p: First performed at the Royal Holborn Theatre (under the management of Miss Fanny Josephs), on Wednesday,m February 17th, 1869 (under the title of "Fettered") A New Original Drama, of great interest, in Threo Acts, entitled A GOLDEN FETTER !

list.item: Scene 1.—A BARBER’S SHOP IN THE VILLAGE OF GRAYTHORPE.
Scene 3.-INTERIOR OF THE OLD HUNTING LODGE.
Scene 1. Bird Fancier's Shop, Great St. Andrew's Street, Seven Dials.
Scene 2. Garden of Mrs. Fortescue’s House, Cheyne Walk, Chelsea.
Scene 3. Exterior of Tom Tit's “Nest of Harmony.”
Scene 4. An interior in Mrs. Fortescue’s House, Cheyne Walk, Chelsea.
Scene 1. - BANKS OF THE THAMES NEAR MARLOW.

body.note: This Drama is the Property of Mr. Lacy.
body.head: A GOLDEN FETTER.

div.head: Scene First.—
div.stage: Before curtain rises sound of storm heard, and as curtain draws up a flash of vivid lightning is seen, followed by a loud clap of thunder—stage divided into two portions — R. portion occupies nearly two-thirds of stage, showing interior of Village Barber's Shop, fitted up with the usual paraphernalia, in addition to which a shelf with several phrenological casts — at back a large window (to open) small panes, before which, with back to audience, a magnificent (male) hair-dresser's dummy—beside this window, and attached to wall a printed bill announcing a "Lecture on Phrenology by J. Nutkins, Esquire"—door and window at side, L.—at door, extending into the second division of scene a barber's pole with pendant basin—L., division, picturesque view of Village of Graythorpe in middle distance—down stage, and nearly facing door of Nutkins' Shop, a sort of covered-in-cart (one of those photographic establishments on wheels), upon which is painted in large letters, "Professor Sloperton and Sun's Photographic Gallery," beneath which inscription a large bill, "Go a-head."

Mooney (a yokel) is being shaved by Nutkins as curtain rises- Several persons holding dripping umbrellas are clustered about door, while others are seen hurrying across stage, at back, as overtaken by storm—heavy rain still heard.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Phew! what a storm! and so sudden too! Well, it's an ill wind that blows no one any good. Beg pardon, Mr. Mooney, but business before pleasure, (he hurries to the door, razor in hand, retaining for a moment his hold of the cloth, so that Mooney is nearly pulled from his seat) How do you do, Mr. Bulb ? How are you, Mr. Wurzel ? Mister Lurcher, your most obedient ? Heavy shower, isn't it ? Best economise time—come in and be shaved—shaved or tittivated—must cut my friends to live, you know: Ha! ha! but my motto is "Cut and come again."

div.stage: Several Villagers enter, they fold their umbrellas, standing "gawking" about.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (bustling about) Here, take this seat, Mr. Bulb, (pulls Mooney suddenly out of chair, and plunges down Bulb in his place—aside) A married man with a large family—twelve heads of hair ! Philoprogenitiveness splendidly developed ! (aloud) Dick will polish you off, Mr. Mooney. (aside) A bachelor, and bald! (calls) Dick! Dick!

div.stage: Enter Dick, door R.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Here I be!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: That boy must have been born with a caul, he answers one so readily. Dick, attend to Mr. Mooney. "His 'prentice hand he tried on man"—ahem! There yon are, Mr. Bulb, done and (whisking away cloth) undone. Smooth as a billiard ball—a chin to be proud of ! Mr. Wurzel, you're called to the chair. (Wurzel takes seat) Don't open your mouth so wide, or you'll swallow the brush, which I can't afford—at a halfpenny.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: (before glass—wiping face) I hope, Mr. Nootkins, this weather wunnot laast, it beant the roight thing—be it? an' Squoire Gilbert a coomin' o' age to-morrer!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (shaving —with pauses) Not to speak of my lecture on phrenology, given gratis, (flourish of razor) for the hedification of all classes.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: That be a queer fancy o' yourn, Measter Nootkins, aboot the bumps.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (shaving rapidly as he talks) Not a bit of it; experience makes perfect, and when it's the question of the heads of the people, who should know better than the barber ?

div.stage: (they laugh—at same time door of photographic cart opens, and Sloperton appears—he stands for some moments on top of steps, glancing up at the sky—he is dressed in a shabby blue military surtout, braided at the seams, much out of elbows, and buttoned up to the chin, except where two or three of the buttons are lost—no shirt collar illuminates the rusty black cravat—a dirty white handkerchief dangles from breast pocket, and an eye glass from neck—hat slightly on one side—he wears long ragged whiskers, moustache, and carries a short cane)

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: Still this cursed rain ! As my partner, the sun, (with gesture upward of hand) is under a cloud for a time, I'll shut up shop for to-day, and have a chat with the barber, (he descends steps and crosses to shop, where he remains carelessly standing, half in and half out of doorway, dandling his cane, and playing with his moustache)

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (busy shaving) What's the last news, neighbours ?

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: Whoi, they do say oop at th' manor house, as Lady Heron lia' made a match vor her son wi' her ward, Miss Lottie Warrener.

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: Then's mother had best stop un hanging about the old hunting lodge. The young squoire be moighty taken wi' the stranger lady, I can tell 'ee.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (whose back is turned to the speaker for the moment, turns quickly, twirling Wurzel, whose, nose he holds at the same time, on chair) You don't say so ?

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: But I do. I ha' spotted 'em often; he a reading poetry books and such loike, and she a angling, (all laugh)

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (twisting about as before) And angling to some purpose if she has caught the squire. Only a touch on the cheek, Mister Wurzel; rather an ornament than otherwise, (to Lurcher) That woman's a mystery if ever there was one ; instead of taking a house in the village, like any other Christian, to think of burying herself in that dreary old lodge, and making acquaintance with nobody.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: She be goin' to leave Graythorpe to-morrow, so there be an end on it.

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: (much surprised) No-o-o-ar?

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: She be off all o' a suddent too, an' farfit's half a year's rent by it.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: She must have lots o' money.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: Oceans on't. When she were payin' me this marnin' for some gardening jobs, I see a roll o' bank notes in her desk, an' a drawer full o' goold an' silver.

div.stage: (Sloperton, who has been listening, lounges into shop, evidently interested)

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: And only one female servant to protect her—bump of cautiousness frightfully deficient.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: She be a ra'al beauty anyhow.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Too much beauty. I don't like it. (all shake their heads) It looks bad in a woman, 'specially when joined with melancholy; pretty women ain't sorrowful for nothing.

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (advancing with a sort of jaunty swagger, and taking centre of shop) May I, who am also a stranger, ask who this lady is, who you are talking about ?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: That's just what we want to know. I should like to examine her head—secretiveness largely developed.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: No one knows who she be, or where she coom fro.'

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: What's her name ?

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: Fortescue. She's a widder.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Another bad sign.

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: But a young widder. She can't be more nor twenty, or twenty-two.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (laughing) A widow, young, rich, and beautiful! Your Mister Gilbert can't be far wrong in hunting her up.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Ah, but you don't know his mother, Lady Heron; she allows nobody to arrange such matters but herself. Pray sit still, Mr. Wurzel, you've turned the edge of my razor; you've destructiveness very large. If I'd such a bump as that I'd use a poultice. (Wurzel rises— Nutkins addresses Sloperton, with wave of hand towards vacant chair) Just a touch of the razor, or a delicate clip of the scissors. No ? another time then—self or friends—always happy to scrape your acquaintance. Why, Dick, haven't you done with Mr. Mooney yet? (aside, as he crosses) Nearly done for him, I fear. Sheet of parchment, lined with red ink. (aloud) Get out! (kicks Dick aside, and takes his place, shaving) Your chin's a rasper, Mr. M. you must have been born with a beard, like an oyster.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (indicating waggon with flourish of cane) Hope, gentlemen, to have the pleasure of taking off some of your heads to-morrow, (to Lurcher) Think you favoured me yesterday ? Portrait gave satisfaction, eh?

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: (sullenly) No—it didn't! It were blacker nor a soot bag.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (airily) Try another ! first impressions aren't always the best, especially in photography, (saluting company with jaunty vagabond air) I take my leave—put your heads together, gentlemen, by to-morrow, and think of the photographer, (he swaggers out, nearly upsetting Mooney, who is mooning at door, crosses stage, goes up steps, and exits into waggon, slamming door after him)

sp.speaker: Bulb.
sp.p: (who with others has risen to look after him) A pleasant sort o' gentleman that.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Gentleman ! Why, Mr. Bulb, where are your bumps ? he's no more a gentleman than you are. Gentlemen don't go about taking people's heads off in carts, (going to door and looking out) I wonder what's become of Tom Tit ? I sent him into the town to get some bills for my lecture nigh four hours ago.

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: He be the stoopidest feller as iver I coom nigh.

sp.speaker: Nutkins.
sp.p: And that's saying a good deal in this village.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: (who is looking out of window, side c.) Here he comes !

sp.speaker: Nutkins.
sp.p: Followed by a parcel o' boys o' course, (they gather about door and window as Tom Tit appears at back of scene, L.—he carries in one hand a birdcage, and holds in the other an umbrella, the latter such an exhibition of looped and windowed raggedness, that as he holds it low down, the first view the audience gets of his face is through a large rent in the umbrella—he pauses at back, and speaks as to persons off scene, L.)

sp.speaker: Tom Tit.
sp.p: Get along wi' yer ! I'm 'shamed on yer, I am !— can't let a poor leetle bird alone ! (addressing audience as he comet down stage, with head half thrust through hole in umbrella) I 'ates boys—I do—they're so rumbustious !

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Come in, Tom, come in out of the wet!

sp.speaker: Lurch.
sp.p: Coom in, stoopid!

sp.speaker: Omnes.
sp.p: Coom in !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (down at footlights and trying in vain to shut umbrella) Thank'ee, yer werry key-'ind, all on yer, but wait till I shuts, my h'umbrella; it aint a good un, but I can't abear to see faults in a friend (shaking it) 'specially one as veeps for its own defects —(enters shop) excuse my droppin' in, in this manner— but—it's a sopper !

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (sharply) I've been waiting for those bills.—What kept you on the road ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: 'Umanity! I vos a peerin' into the 'edges as I kem along, h'admirin' the providence o' nature as shown in blackberries, ven wot should I see a sittin' ker-vite disconsolate on its ve' nest, but this leetle bird— (holding up cage) my 'art was touched.—I took it avay—I could'nt help it— (with rapid change of manner and turning to Nutkins) You're fond o' birds —he's yourn for sixpence!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Sixpence!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Cage included.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (who has taken the cage) Well, it's not dear, (gives money)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Dear! vy it's a loss (aside as he pockets the money) to some vun, for I found the cage as veil—on a vinder sill —

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (who has hung up cage) He must have the bump of benevolence very large somewhere or other! (to customers who are departing) Good day Mr. Lurcher!—good day, Mr. Bulb ! Mr. Mooney, you're going through the window! that's the way out—good day, neighbours! (when all have left he turns to Tom) Tom! get a pot of paste and stick these bills in the village, inside and out.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with a face of perfect idiocy) Stick 'em inside out ?

div.stage: (Dick laughs)

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (aside) What a simpleton it is! (aloud) How could people read 'em ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (same expression) 'Ow should I know ?—both sides is the same to me.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (aside) Poor creature! (aloud) Now go, and keep your eyes open !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I vill. (as Nutkins turns and goes up stage, Tom takes his umbrella, a new one, passes swiftly out of door, opens it with a sly look at Audience)—I 'ates to be taken advantage on! Exit Tom, L.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: (who has been busy at back of shop, comes down stage with wig box) Now, Dick, look after the shop, while I run across to the Manor House. Lady Heron mustn't be disappointed of her new front and Sir Gilbert coming of age tomorrow (pausing at door—coming back)—but where's my umbrella ? (taking up the rag that Tom has left) Well, I declare, if that poor creature hasn't taken my umbrella in place of his own !— what a head he's got! (as he hurries out, opens umbrella and exits, L.) He never thinks of anything!

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: (at door) If I could only see any chaps on the green, I'd be off to have a game at chuck-farthing—(lightning) my eyes ! what a flash! it a'most blinded me! (he retreats into shop)

div.stage: Enter Sir Gilbert Heron, half supporting a cloaked and veiled lady (Florence) L. U. E., they come hastily down stage and enter the shop.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: (pulling off cap) Sir Gilbert !

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (hastily) A chair! a chair! (places one himself, into which the lady sinks) Quick, a glass of water! I hope you have suffered no injury ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: A fright only—when my pony started at the lightning, I kept my seat, but jumped out before the chaise overturned.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: How fortunate I was there ! (he approaches her and hands her a glass of water, but as he would come still nearer she rises hastily as shaking water-drops from her dress, then raises veil of hat)

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: (aside, in great surprise) The lady of the Lodge!

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: It's impossible for you to return in the same chaise— one of the shafts is broken. Can't I persuade you to accept my mother's hospitality for a few hours until––

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (quickly) Oh, no! I must return home at once. (glancing towards Dick) If I could obtain a covered carriage ?

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: (advancing and touching hair) There's one at the "Jolly Reapers."

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (aside) Confound his officiousness !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Go, my good boy, and tell them to get it ready at once.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Yes 'um. (he darts out, and off, L.)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (turns to Sir Gilbert) I shall never be able to thank you sufficiently, Sir Gilbert Heron, for your timely aid: but it seems to be my fate to be always your debtor.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Mine!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p:
(with a forced gaiety of manner, and slightly averting her face, as still occupied with her dress) Since the time I found I was not alone in my taste for angling, and that chance was
always bringing us together in our rambles. Haven't you permitted me to make free with your library, and introduced me to all kinds of pleasant forest companions ? (aside with slight movement towards door) I hope he has found the carriage!

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Florence! (he checks himself) Mrs. Fortescue—till you came, not the forest alone, but the entire world was a blank to me.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Our friendship was a folly! (bitterly) I might have known it couldn't last, (checking him as he is about to speak) No, no; in mercy to yourself, Sir Gilbert, do not raise hopes that can never, never be fulfilled, hopes that in very charity to you I must destroy.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: You forbid me even to hope.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (sadly, placing her hand upon his arm) Sir Gilbert, you mustn't judge me hastily ; there's a shadow upon me—the more terrible because invisible to all but me ! a shadow which blots out each ray of sunshine that steals into my miserable life.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (quickly) Explain this mystery, (he takes her hand)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I cannot! (she withdraws her hand) I dare not! mine is a fate from which there's no escape—no escape but the grave!

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Florence!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (with an effort) Gilbert, this is our last meeting.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Our last?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: My arrangements are already made. To-morrow I quit Graythorpe to return no more. It's the last favour I ask you—aid me to forget!

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (seizing the hand she extends to him, and kissing it passionately) Forget you—never! (as he says this, one of the back windows of the shop is furtively opened, and the head of Tom Tit appears, his mobile face denoting every variety of low cunning and astonishment) Make me at least your confidant. Tell me what is this mystery that surrounds you ? If I mayn't contribute to your happiness, let me share my misery.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Before I leave this place for ever, it's but just you should know something of my sad story, so that you may know how hopeless the gulf that separates us.

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Confide in my love, Florence—my friendship—I implore you !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (in much agitation) Not now ! not now!—Have pity on me, Gilbert, and let me go !

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: I'll call at the Lodge. You'll see me ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p:
(firmly) No. (Sir Gilbert, with a gesture of despair, turns slightly as to move up stage—the window at back closes and Tom's head disappears for a moment) It's for your sake as well
as mine I have come to this decision—but I'll write; yes, I'll write. In an hour from this in the hollow of the old tree, near our angling place, you'll find a letter which will explain all. (moving towards door) The rain has ceased. Don't follow me to the inn !—I'll keep the promise I have made you. Farewell! (she has paused at threshold of door—Gilbert springs towards her)

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Not farewell!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: It must be—farewell for ever!

div.stage: (she passes out, crosses stage hastily and exits up, L.—Tom Tit's head appears also for a moment, peering round corner of ship, as looking after her)

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: For ever!—She cannot mean it!—Can she have heard of my mother's wish respecting my cousin, Lottie Warrener?—My mother! (window softly opens behind) A dozen times I've been on the point of telling her all; but her air of severity—my foolish habit of blind obedience. I know not what has banished all idea of confidence between us, and the nearly uttered secret of my love has died upon my lips, (as he moves towards door, Tom Tit's head becomes more and more visible—suddenly Sir Gilbert pauses, and picks glove from the floor) A glove! Florence's glove ! (kissing it rapturously) I'll keep it till I can claim the hand of its owner. (thrusting it in his breast, he quits shop and exits, R. U. E.)

div.stage: Tom Tit creeps round corner of Nutkin's house.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p:
(looking after him) Vell I never!—To think o' young men agoin' agin the vishes o' their parients in this fashion ! Vot a 'ullabaloo there'd be at the Manor 'Ouse, if Mother Heron knew it ! She's a Tartar!—the cream of all the Tartars! It 'ud kill her with passhin if she know'd it. (to audience) S'pose I tell her! Werry sorry to lay a h'inflammation agin anybody, but ven morals is consarned 'tv'ud be wrong to hesitate—besides, somebody h'else might tell her— and I 'ates to be bowled out in that mean way. (going, he stops) Stop a moment! it don't do to do nothink in a 'urry. (puts umbrella under his arm, and with air of ludicrous thoughtfulness, comes slowly down stage) Ven a cove takes to thinkin' all at vunce, its vonderful 'ow it makes his 'ead ache! The thing for me to do is to connobble that letter; that's a leetle game as must pay anyvays, but there's some arrants as I've got to attend to fust, and it von't do to neglect the ready-money bis'ness. I've got to call upon this 'ere forty-gruffer as lives in a carryvan, like the wild beasties. The butcher's wife isn't satisfied with her portrait, and vants him to take it back, and sell it for somevun else's. Ven I've done with him there's time enough for the billy-ducks; I'll take care to be in time for
the fust delivery, (with a wink at audience, he slinks into shop) My h'eyes! it wur a lucky bit o' bis'ness stumblin' on this 'ere sleepy willage ! It suits my complaint to a nicety ! (takes up something, and drops it suddenly) There's the barber! (looks off) No, it's the boy—(Dick runs across stage and enters shop) Drat boys ! they ain't got no respect for nothink !

div.stage: (Dick stumbling on threshold, drops a sixpence he has in his hand, and it rolls towards Tom Tit, who, with lightning quickness, puts his foot on it.)

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Hullo !—What are you doing here ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with dignity) Minding the shop.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Get off my sixpence ! The lady give it me.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: And this is the way you takes care on it ! I'm 'shamed on yer, I am! Vy don't yer tie it up in yer handkercher, or put it in yer boot ? You can't be too cautious with money— stop, (pushing Dick back and taking up coin) 'Spose, Dick, I keeps it for yer; an' you can ask me for it ven yer vants it! (he pockets coin, and makes for door, with comical look at audience) It's h'awful to trust sich a young child with money, and so many thieves about.

div.stage: He exits at back, pursued by Dick, who seizes his coat-tails just as scene closes in.

div.head: Scene Second.—
div.stage: Interior of Sloperton's Photographic Waggon —door at back, C.—windows at each side of door—the waggon is fitted up very poorly as a photographic place of business— a curtain, L hides a sort of recess.
Sloperton half draws aside curtain and enters carrying a photographic apparatus, which he places, R.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Stand there! and bad luck to you, for you've brought bitter bad luck to me!—Of all the infernal machines that man's folly has invented, you're about the worst:—As if Nature's own handiwork wasn't bad enough, but she must be called upon to repeat her blunders, (coming down stage) Not a farthing taken to day ! London's the only place for a man of my intellect, (indicates photographic machine) However, I thought I'd turn an honest penny till better things turned up. (laughs) An honest penny ! I'd rather make it twopence without the qualification—(taking off coat and hat, and throwing them carelessly on apparatus) I'll put on my working blouse, and give a finishing touch to that flaming poster I've prepared for to-morrow. Each created thing to exist must prey upon some other, while Time, the Avenger, devours us all—"Aureo hamo piscari." (shrugs shoulders) Money's the only bait.

div.stage:
(pushing aside curtain, he re-enters recess, L.—as the curtain closes behind him, the door of the waggon is burst
open, and Tom Tit appears, as mounting steps—he enters with clothes disordered and out of breath as from running —speaking through door, which he nearly closes, and agitating umbrella threateningly, his back to audience)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Be off with yer! yer nasty h'avaricious boy!—to make all this noise about a sixpence!—I never saw yer money!—You must have lost it playing at marbles, (closing door, slips bolt and turns to audience) He's got no witnesses an' no friends—so the transaction's a safe 'un. (speaking as he comes down stage) But where's the h'artist ? h'everybody seems to be attending to somebody h'else's business to-day— it's shameful ! (looking about him) Nothink in my way !— nothing light and portable!—Picturs !—I 'ates picture !— smudges as no vun vill give a h'appeny for ! Rags is better— so's bones ! Fine h'arts!—drat yer h'artful objec's ! (starts violently, as in prying about, his eyes rest upon the photographic machine, coat, hat, &c.) I beg your parding, sir, you're quite mistaken,—I (recovering himself) It's on'y a vooden box on a stick, an' I took it for the proprietor, (advances cautiously towards apparatus—as he does so Sloperton re-appears in blouse behind him)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I vonder vether there's anythink inside?—Anythink as a chap might (as he lifts end of cloth, and is about to peep, Sloperton seizes him by the ear.)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (quiet voice) I've caught you, have I?—What do you want here ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with arm over eyes in a sort of half abject, half bullying trepidation) Caught me, have yer? (writhing about, but without raising eyes, as the other hauls him dawn stage) You've a nasty pryin' natur', you 'ave, whoever yer are ! (sinking on knees near footlights) An' now yer 'ave caught me, vot are you a goin' to do with me? Some mean thing, I'll be bound!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (releasing him with a start of astonishment) Impossible !—1 must be dreaming!—It can't be !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (also with a start) I knows that voice! I'm sure I've seen it somewheres! (he peers up in his sneaking way—their eyes meet—Sloperton releases him, and starts back as Tom Tit rises nimbly to his feel) I'm blest if it isn't Swell Jasper, as was my tip-top pal in the Colonies !

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Young Simplicity!—The devil!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Oh, don't!—don't call names! it ain't perlite, and (glancing round) it might be dangerous, (observing that Sloperton draws back) You wouldn't cut an old friend ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Well, what's been your little game since we parted ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (meekly) I've been doin' the h'onest dodge.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Honest! (laughs) I thought you'd more fixed principles.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Vell, honesty don't pay as it oughter, for all they writes it up ! (sighs) Mine's been a life of trials!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: And of convictions! (laughs) You're thirsty perhaps?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with a sly leer) Lor! how you guesses things !

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: I've a bottle of something in the cupboard: it will open your heart—(aside, as he crosses stage)—and your mouth too.

div.stage: Exits behind curtain with a movement of finger, a sort of— "I'm up to you"—gesture, as he disappears.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (solus.) 'Eres a caper! To think o' Swell Jasper a turnin' up in this h'odd sort of a vay, in this queer lookin' 'ouse on wheels! (with a look of low cunning, he first glances at curtain, then faces round to audience) I vonder vot's his leetle game ? If I on'y dared trust him with vot's been a runnin' in my 'ead since—(stops, and wipes his forehead with coat sleeve) I dussn't do it myself—It makes me all of a prisperation to think of it.

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (re-entering) Think! think of what ? Here, take a thimbleful of this ; it will give you thought and courage both ; (fills glass, which Tom Tit empties) and please to remember that my name's Sloperton—(with mock dignity) Professor Sloperton.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Professor o' what ? (looking around) A h'artist! (with great commiseration) I never thought I should see a man o' your talons come down to that!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: It's been a come down indeed, as far as profit's concerned, (leaning upon Tom Tit's shoulder and filling his glans) I'll open my heart to you!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Do it gently, then—the least think turns me.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: I've only been six months in England, (he pauses)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Go on.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Returned, to find all my relations dead—my –(pauses)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (who has taken bottle, fills) Bless you ! what's relations ? —I never know'd my parients—a pledge of affecshin—left at the vurkus door—a pledge that vos never redeemed.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (gloomily) Hold your tongne!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I can't—not ven you talks o' fathers an' mothers, and sich like luxuries: I'm too susceptible ! (picks his pocket of his handkerchief)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (with bitterness) No fear I shall talk of mine!— sufficient that I was without friends, and without resources. (turning on Tom Tit) You've taken my handkerchief!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (innocently) 'Ave I ? (wiping eyes) I knew your story would affect me !

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: Bah! (walks stage impatiently—Tom Tit hastily thrusts handkerchief into pocket) When men like me become poor, they become dangerous.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (aside) I think I'll trust him!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (stopping before him) Can you tell me how to fill an empty pocket ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: P'raps.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: How?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Stop a bit!—you're so timpestious!—sure there ain't no one behind that curting ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (who leans on photographic machine, watching him) See for yourself.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I will—(peeps—then with rapid change of manner crosses to Sloperton) You're 'ard up!—so am I.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: It's a chronic infirmity with most of us.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: What do you say to a young 'oman as is vurth a lot o' tin! (sinking voice) I ain't been able to sleep o' nights for thinkin' on her. (is taking out handkerchief, but hurriedly puts it back, remembering it is Sloperton's.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (laughing) What, are you in love ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (very briskly) Vith the tin, certingly. It's a safe investment. She lives nearly alone, and her name is––

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Fortescue!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (who is about to whisper in Sloperton's ear, stands aghast) You know her ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (with assumed carelessness) A widow, young and pretty; lives with one female servant in the old Hunting Lodge ; keeps gold and bank notes in a bureau, which at the tap of a crowbar would open in two minutes. Is that the party ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (astounded) And to think he's only been in the willage four-and-twenty hours ! What a 'ead ! what a 'ead !

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: The business is a safe one—plenty of profit and no danger.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: No danger ? (aside) I might ha' done it myself, (aloud) Women shew fight sometimes though.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (as reflecting) Stop, I've arranged it all.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (lifting his hands in wonder) He's harranged it all! What a 'ead ! what a 'ead !

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: If this thing's to be done at all it must be done at once. She leaves Graythorpe to-morrow. You get admission to the Lodge this evening.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Me!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Upon a pretence I've already invented. I've a disguise ready for you.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: For me ? what is it ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (laughs) One that will suit your sweet simplicity to a nicety. Once in the house you open the door or window for me. Ten minutes more—(sinking voice)—and the business will be done and over.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (nervously, and taking a long breath) That's comfortin'! not that I'm afeered; but I 'ates to be ill-conwenienced. (going, and speaking adds) There's no shares in the letter.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Why in such a hurry ? The disguise is here.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Why yer see, there's a little arrand as I've promised to do, and—and—honour, yer know— honour before everything. You needn't lock up the bottle, I'll be back in the twinkling of a bedpost, (he stops at door) Vould you have the goodness to look out and see if there appears to be sich a thing as a boy 'anging about ?

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (moving to window) A boy !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: A leetle wagabone not vorth a sixpence.

div.stage: (as Sloperton opens window and looks out, Tom Tit picks up the brandy bottle, which the former has placed on the floor and thrusts it into pocket)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: There's no one that I can see.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Thank'ee, I never could abear boys, they're so selfish. (opening door)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (as Tom Tit descends) Take care of the ladder and the rope, they may be unpleasant reminders.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (re-appearing above level of floor) I say! don't, don't talk in that vay, I'm so susceptible! (turns head suddenly, as looking down.) Oh! leave go my leg, vill yer? yer young warmint! (strikes down with umbrella, then he disappears as pulled from beneath)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: (laughing) The boy's after him! he'll catch him too. No, Tom's as nimble as a rabbit, and when fear lends wings a greyhound wouldn't catch him. (closes door and comes down stage) This meeting's a lucky one. A desperate disease requires a desperate remedy, and what disease is more desperate than poverty ? My only chance is to get away from England somehow, and then— (taking a pack of cards from pocket and mapping them as he speaks)—with these loose leaves from the devil's prayer-book I can work my way anywhere— that is anywhere but here. He lifts curtain and exits.

div.head: Scene Third.—
div.stage: Interior of a large Antique Room in the Old Hunting Lodge—the windows at back reach to the floor ; they open out upon a balcony, beyond which a rich sweep of woodland is visible—over the tree-tops the moon is seen slowly rising — the furniture of room is massive and ancient—an antique bureau, R. 1 E., on which are writing materials, &c.—on table, a little up stage, R. c, a lamp is being arranged by Martha, an elderly Female Servant.

Florence Fortescue is standing by open window, the moonlight bringing out her pale face and pensive attitude with much
distinctness—several travelling trunks scattered about room, while on floor and chain there is a confusion of shawls, robes, laces, &c.

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: (moving a little from table) Ma'am! (aside) Her mind be far away from here, poor thing! (coughs) Ahem! Ma'am !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (turning her head) Did you speak, Martha?

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: I wanted to know, ma'am, if I'm to go on with the packing ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (coming wearily from window) No; we've time enough. Close the shutters, and leave me alone for a little while, (she crosses to table and begins to turn over lace, &c, mechanically, as Martha closes shutters, and drops bar across them, and draws heavy damask curtain, so as in part to hide window, then crosses to door, L.) I'll call you when I want you. Exit Martha, L. 2 E. Alone at last! (the sinks into chair by table) Solitude is the only sanctuary for the wretched—and who in this world can be more wretched than I am ? Nothing is living with me but the past (her head drops forward upon her hands, she remains thus for a moment, then suddenly rises to her feet, with almost fierceness of manner) Yet what have I done to deserve such a fate. (pacing stage) For years I have kept the secret that destroys me, fast locked in the depths of my own heart, and, living retired from all, had become inured to my silent misery, as the captive in time ceases almost to feel the weight of the fetter or to hear the clank of his chain—but now (she pauses, and resting one hand upon bureau, presses the other painfully to her forehead) I shall go mad! mad !when I think of it! Why did my unhappy fate conduct me to this village ? Have I not boldly faced my sorrow, fought with it, and, as I thought conquered for ever? When—when (with passionate gesture) I love, and am loved ! (walks a few steps, then again pauses with short, self-derisive laugh) Love ! Is it I who dare, even to myself, repeat those words, words so soft and musical to others, so full of terror to me. Alas! nothing is living, nothing should be living in my heart, but the presence of my great despair, (moving up stage towards table and sinking on sofa, she covers her face with her hands—a knocking is heard at door, L. 2 E.—starting to her feet, and hastily brushing away tears) Who's there ?

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: (entering, L. 2 e.) Excuse me, ma'am, but here's some one who wishes to speak to you.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (in alarm) To me ! I told you I would see no one- no one!

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: I said so; but of all the pertinacious

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (alarmed) You have not admitted him ?

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Yes, ma'am. He's such a 'umble, nice spoken body, one couldn't say no to him.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (annoyed) What is his business ?

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: It's something on a charity, ma'am; an appeal for the unfortunate.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Oh, I'll see him then. Shew him in here, and remain with him till I come. (Martha exits, L.) For the unfortunate. To that plea neither door nor heart of mine must be shut.

div.stage: (Exit, R., hurriedly, with handkerchief to her eyes, and almost immediately after, Martha re-enters, l. 2 E., ushering in Tom Tit, he is disguised as a sort of Methodistical street preacher—he is no longer the stupid dolt of the first scene, nor the leering rascal of the second, but a sanctimonious humbug.

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: (to Tom Tit, who rests bashfully just inside door) What name did you say ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (meekly) Magsman—the Reverend Timothy Magsman, as is chairman o' the Society for the Propagation o' the Heathen in Furrin Parts.

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Mrs. Fortescue will see you directly. Take a chair.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Thank'ee, mim; ven I've viped my shoes, (shuffling with feet)

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: (sharply) The mat's outside the door.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (seating himself, and crossing hands over crook of umbrella) Oh, don't bring it in for me, miss.

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Miss! well I'm sure! how old may you take me to be?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Twenty-three, (aside) That's the sort o' buster for the wimen!

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Think o' that now ? (laughs) Why, I shan't see forty again.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (aside) Nor fifty! (wipes eyes, under cover of which action he surveys apartment.)

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Aint you well, sir ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I suffer, (loud sigh) Ah! it's a sweet thing to suffer— it makes us 'umble. (aside) Cus her ! why don't she go? (a bell rings R.—he rises) Wot's that ?

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: Mrs. Fortescue's bell! (going) I'll be back directly.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Dont 'urry yourself, Miss—on my account.

sp.speaker: Martha.
sp.p: What a nice old gentleman. (Exits, R.—Tom Tit, who during the above conversation, has been dexterously stuffing laces, &c, into umbrella as into a carpet bag, springs to his feet, with a nimble briskness and an utter change of manner)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p:
Gone at last! Old women are like plasters, ven vunce they takes they sticks for ever, (he goes quickly up stage, pushes
curtain aside, lifts bar from shutters, as a shrill whistle is heard outside and is about to open portion of latter, when, as alarmed by a footfall, he drops curtain and turns with re-assumption of former manner, towards R. 2. E., by which Florence enters)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I am sorry to keep you waiting, but—(she stops, struck by Tom Tit's peculiar attitude)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: 'Ow beautiful is the voice o' natur in this sylivian spot! (speaking as he comes down stage) I was a listenin' for the whistle o' the blackbird.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Your business ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Vait a minit, mum, and I'm at your service—there (holding umbrella awkwardly between knees he makes desperately comic efforts to pull on a hideously ragged black glove) I know wot's doo to a lady.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: What do you mean.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: My means is limited, mum—werry limited! Hact accordin' to your conshins! that's my maximum, (sighs) And I leaves the rest—(sighs again) to the generosity of the parties.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (with some hauteur) My servant tells me you are here on a mission of charity; be brief, for my time is limited.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Yes, mim, it is charity as has brought me here.' (with rapid change of manner as he approaches close to her) Charity for you.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: For me.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Do you recognise this envelope ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: My letter! Give it me!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Yes, mim; (putting letter in his pocket) but as honesty's a rarity it should be paid for as sich.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (recoiling before his change of manner) Who are you ? What do you want ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with stealthy look around) Hist! (approaching her quickly as she shrinks back towards door) You've no call to be afeared, I'm gentle as a lamb to a lady o' your sex.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (with a cry, as though a new light had broken in upon her) Martha! Martha !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (with sudden leap, placing himself between her and the door, and with a fierceness of manner in strong contrast to his usual cringing tone) I say, none o' that, you know. Don't—don't rouse the lamb! it may be mischeevious. (falling back into former sly sneering tone) If people vill take to writin' vords of more than vun syllabub, they must pay the penalty, which, in this case, is fifty pounds!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (indignantly) Fifty pounds! (aside) Such a confession in such hands ! (aloud) You have mentioned the price?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Fifty; it's a fleabite to you.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Enough; I see you have no conscience ! (she takes lamp from the table and crosses to bureau)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (aside, and watching her movements with nervous impatience) I vish she'd be quick! there's no shares in the fifty, and the professor'll be down on us directly.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (who has deposited lamp on bureau) Will you swear to me that you have no knowledge of the contents of that letter?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (horrified) Oh, mim! You haven't got a book of any kind 'andy, have you ? I'd kiss it directly.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (haughtily) I submit to the extortion, (preparing to unlock drawer) You shall have the reward you deserve.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (aside, and glancing nervously at window, while his fingers are twitching towards bureau—the whole man a picture of trepidation and greed) I might have asked double, (as she opens drawer, the light of the lamp falls over her face and bust, the rest of the stage is in gloom, her back is turned towards window—as with sudden resolve) I will! (he makes a sort of half rush to the bureau) Stop a bit! I — (he is about to lay his hand on Florence's shoulder, when she turns swiftly upon him, and presents a pistol, which she has taken from drawer)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Villain ! give me my letter! (Tom Tit recoils as she advances upon him—sinking upon his knees, he opens, in his fright and desperation, his umbrella, and a perfect avalanche of lace and other articles tumbles about his ears) The letter !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (who has crouched down, rises to his knees and begins to fumble everywhere in his clothes) Ye-e-es, of course; I—I came on purpose. Take—take care !—on'y think, if your hand should shake, and the wepping went off by accident!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: The letter! (as she speaks the shutters at back are pushed silently open, and Sloperton enters room—he stands for a moment unobserved by either—the moonlight falling over his figure)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (rising to his feet as he draws the letter from his pocket) Oh, lor'! oh, lor'! this is the werry meanest thing—here— here it is!

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (coming suddenly between, and snatching the pistol) Allow me!—so white a hand was never meant for such a weapon ! (as Florence turns towards Sloperton, the latter utters a cry and staggers back, like one paralyzed by astonishment—the moonlight is managed as to fall on the group) Florence! Is it possible ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: You ! you ! Jasper Blythe!—come back at last ! (she has staggered back, and now stands in an attitude of mingled fear and horror—one hand resting on bureau, the other extended, as to keep him back)

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p:
(by a perceptible effort recovering his calmness and effrontery) Come back ! And why not ? You have kept your calculation badly! My time being up, I naturally return
to England, and (he laughs) to you. (he makes a movement towards her, but, with a gesture of horror, she avoids him)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Do not touch me, Jasper ! do not dare to touch me! or I alarm the house!

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: The house! (half turning to Tom, who is standing in a state of utter and ludicrous bewilderment) Look to the door, Tom, and let no one enter, (to Florence) Come, come, this meeting is quite accidental. I mean you no harm—listen to reason! (he moves a little up stage and places pistol on table, keeping however between it and Florence)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (faintly) Reason! reason from you! (with effort) Oh, Jasper, Jasper! why have you sought me out ?

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (impatiently) I tell you this meeting is accidental, though devilish lucky as it happens. After so many years separation, it's hard if—(he advances towards her, and as she endeavours to avoid him, throws his arm about her—she shrieks)

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (struggling) Let me go! you shall have all that you want, but don't—don't touch me!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (advancing in alarm) Oh, I say, don't! think—think of the neighbours.

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: (savagely) Back, you fool, and keep the door ! I'll silence her, or––

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (bursting from him) Help! help! (she rushes up stage, and sinks almost at the feet of Sir Gilbert, who leaps in through the window)

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: Florence, Florence, fear nothing ! I will protect you! (he snatches up pistol from table as Sloperton recoils, while Tom sinks limp and fainting against door)

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (raising pistol as Sloperton makes a movement towards him) Advance a step nearer and I fire !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: (springing up with a cry) No! no! (arresting his hand) For my sake, don't fire !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: (shutting his eyes and flattening himself against the door) For all our sakes !

sp.speaker: Sir G.
sp.p: (to Sloperton and putting Florence gently by) Your business here ?

sp.speaker: Sloperton.
sp.p: Really you'd better ask the lady, (turning to Florence, who has covered her face with her hands) Silent ! well then, if I must speak (with change of manner and turning with insolent triumph to Sir Gilbert) I am here by the right of a Husband ! (Sloperton, up stage, r., points derisively to Florence his eyes fixed on Sir Gilbert, who, pale and speechless, up c, stands like one thunderstruck, gazing on Florence, who, with head bent, half cowers as overwhelmed with shame and misery—Tom Tit sinks back against door, L. 1 E., overcome with astonishment, as curtain rapidly descends upon Tableau)

div.head: Scene First.—
div.stage: Door r. ; portion of a verandah, and above that window and balcony of Mrs. Forteseue's house, Cheyne Walk, Chelsea ; at the back of the scene a high garden wall, weather stained, and half covered with ivy; strong nail-studded door in centre, with key in lock.

Sloperton, with arms folded, is leaning against the wall smoking a cigarette; he is elegantly dressed in morning toilette, his whole appearance in strong contrast to the dilapidated swell of the First Act; his cheeks are closely shaven, but he wears beard and thick moustache; his manner is sneeringly polite, and intensely self-possessed. Florence is walking the stage in great agitation; she appears pale and suffering, as from recent illness. As Curtain rises she pauses in centre of the stage and turns towards him.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: When will this persecution cease, Jasper Blythe ? You have had all the money you demanded and more—much more—on your solemn promise that our meeting at Graythorpe was our last.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: The meeting you speak of took place a year ago.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Just a year. I'm not likely to forget its date.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Many things may take place in twelve months to change a former resolve. I changed mine, and so (throwing away cigarette) have returned from Paris to see you. I must have money—a large sum—and at once.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Always the same demand.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: It'll be the last I shall make. In return I promise that you shall be rid of me for ever; I emphasize the word— for ever !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: You are not to be believed; and even if you were, I have no money.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: No ready money, that's possible; but you've jewels, which if––

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Jasper Blythe, have you forgotten the past ? Shall I recall it to your memory ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Quite useless; I make a point of never looking backwards.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: You must and shall ! Poor and an orphan, you were taken into my father's, your uncle's, house—and treated as a son; you repaid him by secretly winning the affections of his daughter, a mere school girl, and––

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p:
We eloped and were married. Your father, not being of the flinty-hearted sort, forgave us; placed me in a position of trust in his counting house, of which trust—(he lights
another cigarette while speaking) owing to circumstances over which I had no control, I grieve to say, I proved unworthy.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Most unworthy. For years, as it was proved, you had been living a life of dissipation, a life of fraud, and your marriage with me was but a heartless plan to screen yourself from my father's anger.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: You wrong the power of your own attractions.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: For my sake, my father would have forgiven you all, when you added another crime to the catalogue—a bill for a heavy amount was forged upon one of my father's clients. He had no longer the power to protect you.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (his smooth calm manner vanishing for a moment)
sp.p: Enough ! let me have no more of this idle talk—granted the crime, I paid the penalty. The law satisfied, I returned to find that your father, keeping true to his old hate of me, had left the bulk of his fortune to a distant relative, leaving you an ample allowance, clogged with the condition that, however, you resumed his name and renounced mine for ever.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I have done so.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: I have renounced it also; (with change of manner) but I have not renounced my hold on you. You can help me.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I cannot.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: You must help me.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I will not.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (seizing her wrist with a momentary outbreak of passion)
sp.p: Take care!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Oh! I have no fear, my misery is too great for that.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Bah! (releasing her and walking stage hurriedly) You're like all the women. You first taunt a man into madness and then play the meek and suffering saint, (pausing in walk) How long have you been in London ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I came here when I left Graythorpe.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: And the young baronet—Does he visit you often ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: I am a woman and defenceless; none but a coward would insult me.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Well, well, you have heard of him—from him?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: His letters were returned unopened. My seclusion here has been unbroken—till now—and I will take measures that it shall not be broken in upon again,

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: What do you mean ?

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: That I will remove from this place, to fly I know not—I care not where, so that you cannot find me.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: You will find hide-and-seek a losing game to play with me, Florence.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Then will I pray that, when we do meet, the barrier of death may arise between us ! (moving to steps and pausing) Farewell, Jasper !

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (who has made a fierce and rapid movement as to stop her, pauses abruptly as door closes; by a powerful effort he regains his mask of calmness, and with a low whistle, shrugs his shoulders)
sp.p: Well, there's no teaching a woman reason. It was your last chance, Floy—your last! You stand between me and a great fortune—between me and a woman I love—yes, love! A woman who'd place a fortune at my command, with whom I might begin a new life in a new world, who, ignorant of the past, cannot reproach me with it. Between that woman and myself there is but one obstacle—but one! (he has crossed to door, and while he speaks lays his hand on the lock, then with a gesture half of menace, he turns to the window) We shall meet again— we must meet again, Florence Blythe, and that before many hours are over.

div.stage: He exits through door in garden wall, and scene opens discovering—

div.head: Scene Second.—
div.stage: Interior of Bird Shop, St. Andrew's Street, St. Giles's—practical door at back opening into street (as this scene has to open upon an important set it must be left to the scenic artist to paint and otherwise arrange the requisite details of the bird fancier's shop—so as not to interfere with the rapid change. When scene opens Tom Tit is discovered seated on a chair—in one hand he holds a short cane, in the other a piece of bread—standing before him on its hind legs in a French poodle —the poodle makes a snap at bread and Tom gives him a smart tap with the cane.)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p:
Ah! would yer! Is them your French manners ? Snapping at things as don't belong to ye ! If half the pains had been took with my edication as they've took with yourn, I might have been a member of Parliament or some such notoriety, (rising from seat) I found you In Regent Street— close at the heels of a young lady, and as she took no notice of yer, I did. I took yer 'ome to share my crust, and expects to make a five pun' note out of you afore I've done. Mauley's sent word as you can be heered on through me. Ah ! there's no place like London for a chap as has his leetle dodges; and to think but for the fifty I got out of Swell Jasper, I might still be a runnin' of arrants in that cussed village o'Graythorpe. (he is giving dog the bread, but takes it away very quickly, and pops it into his own mouth as a loud tapping is heard on the half glazed door at back (this door has a dirty wire blind) A customer p'raps! (rising and snatching up dog) So I must shut
you up, my beauty! Ah! would yer! If yer does that agin I'll gut yer and stuff yer !

div.stage: Exit with dog, R., and returns immediately—at the same moment Nutkins enters shop, in his usual bright, brisk manner—he is dressed in plain black suit—the undress of a superior servant in a nobleman's family.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: This must be the shop where I am to enquire about Miss Warrener's dog. (looking at a paper) Great St. Andrew's Street, Holborn, yes, I'm all right.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (who has started back with an aspect of intense disgust)
sp.p: Cuss me! if it isn't that friendly illogical barber ! (he turns his back towards Nutkins, at the same time drawing out large cotton handkerchief and half covers his face with it as suffering from toothache)

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Ahem !—I say!—here—he don't hear !— Are you deaf?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Slightually.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: And suffering with toothache (approaching nearer—he starts back) It can't be !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (startled into momentary forgetfulness and turning round)
sp.p: Wot can't be ?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Ha! ha! I thought it was. I'd have sworn to your bumps anywhere—you've such a peculiar set of them. But (looking round shop) What are you doing here?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Minding my own bis'ness.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Your business ! Where did the money come from ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: From my father, (aside) Now for a buster ! (with dignity) I come into his property.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: A father! you !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Why not ? ain't I as much right to a father as h'anybody else ? fathers are common enough, I s'pose!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: They must be.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: What's your business here ?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: A dog ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I ain't seed one.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Then I must have made a mistake, (movement to door)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: You don't mean a kind of a sort of a French poodle?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Of course I do; Miss Warrener, Lady Heron's niece, lost it yesterday in Regent Street.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Lady Eron ! what have you to do with her ?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: She couldn't do without me. Her ladyship, you see had quarrelled with Sir Gilbert, but she got frightened when she heard of his wild doings in London, and set me to find news of him. She knew I shared her anxiety.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Wot's he got to do with you?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Why, you see, he twined himself round my heart by his hair! I curled him for his first party and cut him regularly for years, (with emotion) Such links ain't easily broken !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Oh ! stow that! wot about the daug?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Her ladyship and Miss W. are drivin' here themselves; she will be astonished at seein' you—astonished and pleased ! (noise as of carriage stopping at door) Here they are !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Pleased! (brightening up) Arter all, she knows nothing agin me. P'raps I can sell her a bird ! I'll try to sell her somehow ! (with sudden alacrity, he rushes up stage, nearly upsetting Nutkins who is opening door) Stand back, will yer ! (as he places himself right before him) a thrustin' your­self in other people's way. (throwing open door with eager servility, as Lady Heron sweeps in, followed by Lottie Warrener) Walk in, your ladyship! walk into this 'umble abode of natur'. It's a welkim h'onor to see you here, (placing chair upon which her ladyship sits) It's a wonder the birds doesn't burst out a singin' all at vunce.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.stage: (who has been surveying Tom Tit through her glass, aside to Nutkins)
sp.p: What a queer creature ! for sale, I suppose.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: A child in heart, Miss Lottie, a child in heart!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.stage: (to Tom Tit, who is behaving most ludicrously)
sp.p: Ha, I think I remember you ! You're the man that interested yourself about Sir Gilbert.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Yes, ma'am—your ladyship ! I felt for him as a son. (Lady Heron draws herself up with a start which quite upsets him) I mean as a brother, (Lottie laughs) or a sister.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: You did your duty, and will have your reward––

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Eh!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: In a good conscience.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: He requires no more.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Certainly— certainly; a good conscience is a sweet thing I It's like grease to boots—that's wot it is, your ladyship, it softens the huppers, and makes the road of life come easy, (aside) I'll be even with that bumptious barber!

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Hadn't we better come to business, Aunty ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: That's it, Miss, let's come to business.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Yesterday, a rascal stole my poodle. (Tom Tit shakes his head sadly) They tell us we can get it back through you.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: What's the price the thief demands ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p:
Price !—Um !(looking doubtfully from one to the other)
I saw the rascal this mornin'—he's a 'ard mouthed 'un, he is, he talked of five pounds.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Nonsense !

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Five fiddlesticks, (aside, and crossing to door as glancing out) I wonder whether Captain Graham will come ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Oh, mum! think o' the trouble he had in stealin' it.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: What!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Don't!—Oh, please don't, yer la'ship. The way that 'ardened ruffian talked this mornin', have shaken my narves to pieces.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: What did he say ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: He said as how a French poodle was a most useful cretur',—when you're tired of him as a dog, you've only to cut off his legs and scoop out his inside to make a muff of him. (both ladies scream) And when he said it, his looks was hawful. (as he says the last word, he stands between the two ladies—both draw from their purses a bank note, and extend it towards him)

sp.speaker: Both.
sp.p: Save him! save him!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (briskly, and taking a note with each hand, and with lightning like rapidity thrusting them in pockets)
sp.p: Ven you touches my 'art, I can't say no to nothink.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.stage: (turning to Nutkins, who is regarding Tom Tit with a sort of fond pity)
sp.p: Have you found out Mrs. Fortescue's address ?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Yes, your ladyship.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Eh!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: I saw her by accident in Brompton, and knowing your ladyship's wish, followed her. (gives slip of paper)

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.stage: (to Tom Tit, who is eagerly stretching his neck to hear)
sp.p: We wish to be alone for a few minutes.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: But the shop?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: You can mind it as well outside as in—go!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Directly, your ladyship, (aside, viciously) You won't keep a dog in your 'ouse long !

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: And Nutkins,

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Your ladyship ?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Go home.

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Yes, your ladyship.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (aside and viciously jostling against Nutkins as they are passing out of door)
sp.p: I 'ates such meanness !

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.stage: (delighted, and reading paper which Lady Heron hands to her)
sp.p:
"Cheyne Walk, Chelsea. " Oh, I'm so glad! I always loved old Floy as a sister—a good, kind, duck and darling of a sister, (laughs merrily) I used to call her "old
Floy" because she was four years older than me. She did all my lessons for me at school. I never troubled myself to learn one of them—she was a perfect brick!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: My dear Miss Warrener, I really must trouble you not to offend my ears with slang expressions. I can assure you when I was a girl––

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: More extracts from Ancient History! (aloud and coaxingly) I won't do it again—that is, I'll try not; only you're so down upon me—you're always shutting me up.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: There again! shutting you up.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Well, pulling me up short! you never give me my head a minute, (aside) I'll go and see Florence to night, though, that I will—that I will!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: I confess that I was more than astonished to find by the portrait you have so carefully preserved, of your schoolfellow, that Florence Fortescue, the lady of the Lodge, was the daughter of your father's old friend and London agent, John Fortescue, the banker.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Was cousin Gilbert very spoons on her?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Spoons! Good gracious!

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Oh,—in love then?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Enough to ruin himself, and break his mother's heart! What at that time I thought of Florence Fortescue, I said—I said it harshly, perhaps too harshly ; but was that any reason he should leave my house, and refuse to re-enter it ? (she puts handkerchief to eyes)

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: I think it was for my sake he kept away. You insisted we should make a match of it, and cousin Gilbert didn't see it; and I didn't see it either.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.stage: (snappishly, and with wave of her handkerchief)
sp.p: See it! In my time young people allowed their elders to see for them.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Money's not everything, aunty; I don't care for it.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: P'raps not. At your age you're not expected to know its value. To-morrow I will see Mrs. Fortescue.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: And I to-night, for poor old Gilbert's sake.

div.stage: As she speaks the shop door opens, and Sloperton enters, followed by Tom Tit, who seems both alarmed and astonished—
Sloperton, with affected surprise, raises his hat on seeing Lottie.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Miss Warrener ! This is an unexpected pleasure!

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Oh ! I'm so glad to see you—(aside) I knew he'd turn up. (turning to Lady Heron) Have you forgotten Captain Graham, who was so kind to us in Germany ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Graham ! Captain Graham!!! Oh! what a 'ead! what a 'ead he's got!!

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: Oh! yes, I remember. Captain Graham performed some slight service, which I believe, he was thanked for.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Slight service ! Why, when the boat overturned he saved me from drowning at the risk of his life—I shall never forget it! You must come and see us, Captain ; my aunt will be delighted—won't you, aunty? (Lady H. bows, but very stiffly) To think of our meeting in this queer place! I've lost my little dog, you know, and—and––

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: And this honest fellow, with whom I am in treaty for some sporting dogs, has promised to get it back for you—you can depend upon him. (aside to Tom Tit) Get the old lady away for a few moments. Do you hear?

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.stage: (who has been examining cages through her glass, turns to Tom Tit —haughtily)
sp.p: Have you any foreign birds ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Furrin—lots ! If your ladyship will step into the back shop, I'll shew you some Java sparrers— beauties! (aside, as he conducts her off) I caught 'em, and painted 'em myself, (as they go off, R., Sloperton's careless manner changes into one of eager entreaty as he approaches close to Lottie's side)

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Lottie, have you forgotten the promise you made me at Homberg?

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Promise ! What promise ? I'm sure, sir, I don't remember anything about it.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Oh ! don't trifle with me, darling—you know I love you, and––

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.stage: (putting hand to his mouth, and looking frightened)
sp.p: Hush—you must get my aunt's consent.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: But you are of age, and your own mistress! Lady Heron would secure your fortune for Sir Gilbert—she

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Stop now! I won't have you speak against aunty. You must overcome her dislike to you—it s the only obstacle.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: And Sir Gilbert—what of him ?

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Avoids his mother's house—avoids me.

sp.speaker: Lady H.
sp.p: I will take the birds on your recommendation.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Oh, I'll warrant the colours to stand—I mean, I'll warrant the health of the leetle creeturs—bless 'em t

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Good bye, Captain Graham, we shall expect your visit—(aside, as she passes him) To-morrow.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Permit me to conduct your ladyship to the carriage.

div.stage: As he exits with Lady Heron on his arm, Tom Tit, who has been looking on open-mouthed, sinks in chair at side, R.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Well, this beats everything! It's enough to turn a feller's hair preema-toorarloora-ly grey to see him a-doing of it!

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (speaking as he re-enters, and glancing after carriage, which is heard to drive off)
sp.p: You dislike me, my Lady Heron ; but I will remove that obstacle, as I will remove every other that stands between me and Lottie Warrener ! (he closes door—speaking between his set teeth, and with a dark frown) Yes, I will remove them—every one! (seeing Tom Tit, who has picked up a slip of paper) What's that ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: 'Ow should I know, I can't read ! (giving paper, and watching Sloperton's face with a look of eager cunning) It dropped from her ladyship's muff.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Mrs. Fortescue, Cheyne Walk, Chelsea! (he sluggers as one shot, then, recovering himself, seizes Tom Tit by the collar) You rascal! this is some of your underhand work—confess it! Do you hear? confess it!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Confess what ? that prying barber brought the paper, and they'd the imperence to turn me out while they read it.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (releasing him and speaking in great agitation)
sp.p: Should Lady Heron and Florence meet, all's over with me. It's a desperate game I'm playing—yet—but for her (with bitter emphasis) this woman who hates me, I hold the winning card in my hand, (walking stage) I've done bad things before now. desperately bad things, and for a smaller stake than the hand of Lottie Warrener.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (who has been hopping about endeavouring to catch the meaning of his disjointed words, now speaks with intense astonishment)
sp.p: Why, you can't be in love, Captain ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (pausing, and speaking more to himself than to Tom Tit)
sp.p: Madly.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: In love ! (he has approached close, to Sloperton and glances sideways at him with a look of intense cunning) With how much ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: Oh! she's rich enough! but I should have loved her even without a penny. She believes me everything that's good and noble.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: You don't say so! Well, women believe anything! Does she know anything of the leetle obstacle ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Phew! but should she know ?

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.p: That is my business, (aside) I will take measures to prevent that, (after a pause of reflection) I'll go round to Ned Mauley's, I suppose I shall find our friend Tony the locksmith there ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Sure! He's backing a dawg of his for the rattin'.

sp.speaker: Sloper.
sp.stage: (standing at door which he flings open)
sp.p:
Tony's the
man I want, (to Tom Tit) Shut up this crib and come after me if you like, (aside, as he exits) And now Florence, the game lies between you and me! You've pushed me into a corner, and I'm more than desperate !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Shut up an' come after you! Thank'ee! I'm much obleeged ! (moving to side, he pauses) What a 'ead he's got! a'most too much for one man, that it is! he's sharp vichever vay you touches him, like a fifteen-bladed pen'nife. (with great slyness) I'm afeared Swell Jasper vants to eat his turnip vithout me ! (taking up chair and preparing to go off) but as the field's open to all, and I can't abear meanness p'raps I'll contrive to have my leetle turnip too.

div.stage: Exit and scene closes in showing—

div.head: Scene Third.—
div.stage: Exterior of Tom Tit's Bird Shop, St. Giles's— evening far advanced, all the shutters put up but two—Tom Tit comes out with a shutter, which he prepares to put in its place.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I'd give a bullfinch now—and a pair of finches to to know what game Swell Jasper's up to! Something nasty I'll be bound, (puts up shutter) It's 'orrible to think of his going into anthink of that kind without me! I can't abear sich meanness.

div.stage: Exits into shop to fetch another shutter—Flitter enters, R.

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: To think of that fool Gripper, losing sight of them after all, in that drunken scuffle at the door. What on earth are they up to with those false keys ? We can do nothing till we get the telegram from the police at Homburg; a terrible loss of time, for when Blythe's taken a thing in hand, he's not the man to let the grass grow under his feet. If I can throw this bird fancying-fellow (jerking thumb to shop) off his guard, I may run down the other with his help, (as he speaks Tom Tit comes out of shop without perceiving him, and puts up other shutter)

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: It's sure to be a good thing—if he puts his hand to it. (giving shutter a spiteful kid) Well, I am rasped !

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.stage: (who has approached softly, now places his hand on Tom Tit's shoulder—his manner has changed to one of great simplicity, and he speaks with a country accent)
sp.p: My good mon !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Oh ! blow you and your good man! Wot do yer mean by creepin' up to a feller that way?

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: I'm quite a stranger in London. Is this Great St. Andrew's Street ?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Well, it ain't Regent Street, if that's any help to yer!

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: I want a Mr. Thomas Tit.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: What for ?

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: He's strongly recommended to my notice.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: By who ?

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: Mr. Mauley of the Hand and Glove.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Sorry to say Mr. Tit's not at 'ome—at present!—but I'll introduce' him to yer—(pauses and looks at Flitter side­ways) for sixpence.

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: Sixpence! you're joking!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: If sixpence is a joke, take it serious and make it a shillin'—(Flitter gives money—Tom Tit pockets it eagerly, and draws himself up with great dignity) It's ME. I'm 'im. (aside) Sell No. 1, for the yokel ! Vot do yer want?

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: To buy some dogs, (takes plain gold snuffbox, and takes snuff ostentatiously) It's on commission, so price is no object.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Price no object? Do you happen to want a French poodle? (aside) I'll sell him her ladyship's, and stuff her another!

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: That's my name, (gives card) Oxley, Jacob Oxley! Call at my hotel to-morrow morning early—very early—and we'll come to business.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I'll do yer the pleasure, (aside) He's not fit to go about alone.

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: I shall have the telegram from Homburg by that time, (aloud) Which is my way into Holborn?

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: Straight on, and fust to your left.

sp.speaker: Flit.
sp.p: You'll not forget—early to-morrow !

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p:
I'll bring the poodle, he's a beauty ! (aside) Poor cretur ! it goes to my werry 'art to h'operate on sich a werry easy cove, (producing, with a wink of intense satisfaction, Flitter's snuff box, and a small parcel tied up in a handkerchief, which he has also taken from Flitter's pocket while talking) It's not my fault if I doesn't do the right thing! Somevun's al'ays a-shovin' temptation in my vay! I thinks they does it a-purpose, that I do. (examining box) The 'all mark ! vich o' course means it's all right. The cretur' wil never suspec' me—never. Now what's in the 'andkerchief ! (shaking it, it jingles—Tom Tit's visage brightens into a grin) It sounds like spoons! Vy rot's the old gent been up to! (speaking as he open handkerchief) If ever yer wants to know a man's karacter, see wot he's got in his pockets! (with a cry, he staggers back, as he draws out and holds up a pair of hand­cuffs) A pair o' darbies ! Oh! my precious nerves! (still holding handcuffs, he instinctively thrusts wrist in one hole, as he sinks back half fainting against wall) Of all the mean adwantages, this it, the wust! To think that a poor cove can't put his hands
anyveres now-a-days vithout comin' on somethin' disagree- able!—an' they talks o' betterin' the virkin' classes!

sp.speaker: Nutkins.
sp.p: Keep the cab there, I'll be back in a minute. (entering gaily) Oh! there you are, Tom!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (aside, and hiding handcuffs behind his back)
sp.p: It's the bumptious barber !

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: Lady Heron has a grand pic-nic coming off to-morrow—(flapping him on shoulder) a pic-nic on the banks of the Thames, and I want you!

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: What for?

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: To help unpack the hampers and look after the plate.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: I'm your man!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: It's a sovereign a day, with unlimited victuals.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.p: That's me!

sp.speaker: Nut.
sp.p: I knew you'd be grateful.

sp.speaker: Tom.
sp.stage: (who with much trouble has succeeded in thrusting the handcuffs into Nutkins's pocket)
sp.p: I ham. (with a sigh of intense relief) That's a load horf my 'ands, at any rate, (with glance and gesture which takes the audience into his confidence, he exits after Nutkins, L.)

div.head: Scene Fourth.—
div.stage: Interior of Sleeping Aparment in Mrs. Fortescue's House, Chelsea. Up stage, C, a French bed, muslin curtains drawn; R. of bed (back) a window opening upon a balcony, curtains also drawn ; the moonlight to shine so strongly through as to shew clearly defined shadows of frame­work of window, and the outside balcony upon the curtain; L. of bed (back) small table, with night lamp, sleeping draught, &c.; up stage, R. and L., doors; down stage, R., an escritoire, writing materials, &c.; on escritoire a buhl casket; downstage, L., fireplace; clock on mantelpiece; looking glass, ornaments, &c.; easy chair, ottoman, footstool, and large skin rug, &c, &c; on mantelpiece a lighted lamp, so placed as to throw its light full on chair.

Florence is seated in easy chair, in dressing gown, very palet her appearance weak and suffering. Lottie Warrener seated on ottoman at her feet, cuddling close to her, with a bright schoolgirl-like affection.

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Oh, I am so awfully glad to see you again, yon dear, dear, dear old Floy. I waited till auntie was off to the opera with that horrible bore, Lady Frumpleigh, then I whisked on my hat, and out I bolted.

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: And you're sure Lady Heron won't be angry ?

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p:
Oh, no! and if she is, I on talk her over; she's not a bad sort if you know how to manage her. But to think of your being a widow, Floy! Why, it seems only the other
day you were helping me in those horrid French exercises at Miss Montflather's. How awfully you must have felt it when you lost him ! By the way, I'm caught!

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.p: Caught! What do you mean ?

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: In love you know! You mustn't mind my funny way of talking. When I left our fashionable boarding-school I went down to my uncle's at Melton Mowbray, and ran about wild with George and Harry, my cousins you know, so I picked up some of their talk—it does bother aunt so—she's so stuck up! (Florence raises her finger) Well, I mean, she has such immense notions of birth, position, money and all that sort of thing, (breaking off with a long sigh) Heigho! —but I'm really in love—really !—really !—really !

sp.speaker: Flor.
sp.stage: (speaking faintly and bending her head over Lottie's hand, which she holds in her own upon her knee)
sp.p: With your Cousin, Sir Gilbert, of course ?

sp.speaker: Lottie.
sp.p: Of course not! Gilbert couldn't be more indifferent to me than if we were really man and wife and had been married these five years.
